back: Never trust, always verify. Requires continuous verification, least privilege, and policy-driven access.
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
Aggregates, correlates, and alerts on logs/security events.
Automates incident response workflows and reduces manual effort.
Prevents sensitive data from leaving the organization without authorization.
Controls network access by authorizing users/devices and checking security posture.
Provides integrity; detects data changes.
Adds random data before hashing to prevent rainbow table attacks.
Primary defense against SQL injection.
Use HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, and session timeout to protect session tokens.
Authentication (verify identity), Authorization (permissions), Accounting (logging/auditing).
Ensures a party cannot deny actions; uses digital signatures and audit logs.
Same key for encryption/decryption. Fast. Examples: AES, DES, 3DES.
Public and private key pairs. Slower but enables digital signatures. Examples: RSA, ECC.
System of CAs, certificates, and key management for secure digital communications.
Binds public key to identity. Issued by CA. Contains subject, issuer, serial number, validity dates.
Issues, manages, and revokes digital certificates. Trusted third party.
List of revoked certificates before expiration; used by clients to verify certificate validity.
Online Certificate Status Protocol. Real-time alternative to CRL for certificate validation.
Protocols for secure data transmission over networks. TLS 1.2+ is current standard.
Requires two or more verification methods: something you know, have, are, or location.
Authenticate once to access multiple applications/systems.
Lightweight Directory Access Protocol. Used for centralized user directory and authentication.
Network authentication protocol using tickets; common in Windows domains (Active Directory).
Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service. Centralizes authentication for network access.
Terminal Access Controller Access Control System. Separates authentication, authorization, accounting.
Authorization framework; allows third-party apps to access resources without sharing passwords.
Security Assertion Markup Language. XML-based SSO for federated identity across domains.
Users/systems get minimum permissions needed to perform their role.
Access decisions based on user roles; simplifies permission management.
Fine-grained access control using policies with attributes (user, resource, environment).
Specifies which users/systems can access a resource and what actions are allowed.
Network security device filtering traffic based on rules; can be stateless or stateful.
Monitors network/system for suspicious activity; alerts on detection. Does not block traffic.
Active IDS; detects and automatically blocks suspicious traffic.
Monitors entire network segments; placed at network perimeter.
Monitors individual system logs and behavior; installed on each host.
Web Application Firewall. Protects web apps from attacks like SQL injection, XSS, DDoS.
Intermediary between client and server; can filter, cache, and control traffic.
Sits between clients and external servers; filters outbound traffic.
Sits between external clients and internal servers; provides load balancing and protection.
Virtual Private Network. Encrypts traffic and tunnels through untrusted networks securely.
Connects entire networks/branches securely over public internet.
Allows individual users to securely connect to corporate network from remote locations.
Protocol suite for VPN; encrypts and authenticates IP packets. Operates at Layer 3.
VPN using SSL/TLS protocols; browser-based or client-based remote access.
Decoy system to lure attackers, detect intrusions, and gather threat intelligence.
Network of honeypots; creates entire decoy network for advanced threat research.
Fake credentials/data planted to detect unauthorized access or misuse.
SIEM: logs/correlates; IDS: detects intrusions. SIEM is broader and includes analysis.
Weakness in system that can be exploited; may or may not have known exploits.
Potential cause of harm to assets; comes from threat actors or natural disasters.
Likelihood × Impact; the probability a threat will exploit a vulnerability.
Process of identifying, analyzing, and evaluating risks to prioritize mitigation.
Uses descriptors (high, medium, low) to rank risks; less precise but faster.
Uses numerical values (dollars, percentages); precise but data-intensive.
Implement controls to reduce risk probability or impact.
Transfer risk to third party via insurance, outsourcing, or SLAs.
Acknowledge risk and choose not to mitigate; document decision.
Eliminate the activity or system that poses the risk.
Identifies critical business functions and assesses disruption impact; determines RTO/RPO.
Maximum acceptable time to restore a system/service after disruption.
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time; defines backup frequency.
Procedures to restore critical systems/data after major outages or disasters.
Broader than DRP; ensures business operations continue during disruptions.
Backup facility with infrastructure but no data/systems; slowest recovery.
Backup facility with some infrastructure and data; medium recovery time.
Fully redundant facility with current data; near-instantaneous failover.
Mean Time to Detect; average time to identify a security incident.
Mean Time To Repair/Recover; average time to restore service after failure.
Mean Time To Failure; average time between system failures.
Measured as uptime percentage; formula: MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR).
Malicious software; includes viruses, trojans, ransomware, spyware, worms.
Self-replicating malware attached to files; requires host program to execute.
Self-replicating malware that spreads independently without host file; consumes bandwidth.
Disguised as legitimate software; does not replicate; requires user execution.
Encrypts files/systems and demands payment; delivered via phishing or RDP exploits.
Monitors user activity (keystrokes, browsing) and exfiltrates data without consent.
Malware with kernel/admin privileges; hides itself and other malware; very difficult to detect.
Network of compromised devices (bots) controlled remotely by attacker (botmaster).
Infrastructure attacker uses to communicate with and control malware/botnets.
Social engineering via email/SMS impersonating trusted entity to steal credentials.
Targeted phishing attack against specific individual or organization with customized content.
Spear phishing targeting high-value targets (executives, C-level).
Voice phishing; attacker calls victim posing as authority to extract sensitive info.
SMS phishing; malicious text messages with links or requests for information.
Attackers impersonate executives to trick employees into transferring funds or data.
Manipulating people to divulge confidential information or perform unsafe actions.
Creating false scenario/pretense to gain trust and extract information.
Entices user with something appealing (USB drive, prize) to execute malware.
Unauthorized person follows authorized employee through secured access point.
Searching trash for discarded documents/info containing sensitive data.
Observing user entering passwords/PINs over their shoulder.
Attacker inserts malicious SQL code into input fields to manipulate database queries.
Attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by victims; executes in browser.
Tricks authenticated user into performing unintended actions on another website.
Attacker inserts OS commands into input fields; application executes them with its privileges.
Attackers write excessive data into buffer, corrupting adjacent memory; enables code execution.
Attacker intercepts and may alter communications between two parties.
Attacker captures and resends valid messages/authentication tokens to gain unauthorized access.
Attacker tries all possible password/key combinations; slow but guaranteed to succeed eventually.
Attacker uses list of common passwords/words; faster than brute force but limited scope.
Attacker uses leaked password databases to attempt login on other sites.
Precomputed hash table for quick password cracking; defeated by salting.
Attacker floods target with traffic/requests to exhaust resources and deny legitimate users access.
DoS attack from multiple sources (botnet); harder to block; more powerful than single-source DoS.
Attacker gains higher-level permissions/access than initially granted.
Attacker moves from compromised system to other systems on the same network.
Attacker establishes mechanism to maintain access even after reboot/password change.
Unauthorized transfer of sensitive data out of organization.
Sophisticated, targeted attack by skilled adversaries; maintains long-term presence.
Artifacts suggesting breach: IP addresses, file hashes, domains, email headers.
All possible entry points/methods attackers can use to breach an organization.
Entity conducting the attack; categories: script kiddie, hacker, organized crime, nation-state.
Government-backed threat actor; highly resourced, sophisticated, focused on espionage/sabotage.
Malicious or negligent employee/contractor with legitimate access causing damage.
Attacker targets vendor/supplier to compromise their customers; examples: SolarWinds, 3CX.
Procedures for detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from security incidents.
Preparation, Detection/Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-Incident Review.
Short-term (isolate system), Long-term (fix without losing evidence). Balances speed vs. evidence.
Documented process of who accessed evidence, when, and why; maintains forensic integrity.
Collect, preserve, analyze digital evidence from systems/devices following legal procedures.
Device preventing accidental data modification during forensic analysis.
Dividing network into smaller subnets/zones to limit lateral movement and contain breaches.
Demilitarized Zone; buffer between internal network and internet; hosts public-facing services.
Virtual LAN; logically separates network segments; provides isolation and security.
Physically isolated from other networks; no direct connectivity; highest security but least convenient.
Multiple layers of security controls to prevent/detect/respond to attacks at different levels.
Process of regularly updating software/firmware to fix vulnerabilities and bugs.
Automated tool (Nessus, OpenVAS) identifies known vulnerabilities in systems/applications.
Authorized simulated attack to identify exploitable vulnerabilities; goes deeper than scanning.

front: Difference between Vulnerability Scan and Pen Test
back: Scan: automated, identifies known vulns. Pen test: manual, exploits vulns, tests defenses.
Categorizing data by sensitivity: Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted/Top Secret.
Data identifying individuals: SSN, credit cards, passport, biometrics, location.
Health data regulated by HIPAA; includes medical records, diagnoses, payment info.
Standard protecting cardholder data; requires encryption, access control, logging, regular audits.
General Data Protection Regulation (EU); protects personal data; hefty fines for violations.
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (US); protects health information.
Service Organization Control 2; audits service providers' security, availability, processing integrity.
International standard for information security management systems (ISMS).
Guidelines for managing cybersecurity risk; five functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
Prioritized set of actionable practices (18 controls) to defend against cyber attacks.
Encrypts stored data; protects against unauthorized access if physical storage is stolen.
Encrypts data being transmitted over networks; uses TLS, IPSec, VPN.
Hides sensitive data with characters/symbols; protects PII in non-production environments.
Replaces sensitive data with random token; original data stored securely elsewhere.
Detects and prevents unauthorized data exfiltration via email, USB, cloud.
Regular copies of data stored separately; critical for disaster recovery and ransomware defense.
Backs up only changed data since last backup; space-efficient but slower to restore.
Backs up data changed since last full backup; middle ground between incremental and full.
Backs up all data; large size but fastest restore; forms baseline for incremental/differential.
Keep 3 copies of data: 2 on different media types, 1 offsite.
Backup cannot be modified or deleted (temporarily or permanently); protects against ransomware.
Shared responsibility model: provider secures infrastructure; customer secures data/access.
Cloud provider manages hardware; customer manages OS, apps, data. Example: AWS EC2.
Provider manages infrastructure and platform; customer manages apps and data. Example: Heroku.
Provider manages everything; customer only uses the app. Example: Office 365, Salesforce.
Use strong authentication, encryption, access controls; avoid misconfiguration.
Secure Docker/container images: scan for vulns, use least privilege, control runtime.
Secure container orchestration: RBAC, network policies, pod security policies, secrets management.
Protect APIs: rate limiting, authentication, input validation, secure coding practices.
Secure mobile devices: MDM, app vetting, encryption, strong authentication, remote wipe capability.
Centralized tool to manage mobile devices: enforce policies, deploy apps, remote lock/wipe.
Security software on end devices: antivirus, antimalware, firewall, intrusion prevention.
Integrated security platform correlating data from multiple sources (endpoint, network, cloud).
Aggregates and analyzes logs from all sources to detect and investigate security incidents.
Tool on endpoints detecting/investigating suspicious behavior; enables threat hunting.
Automates incident response: collects data, correlates events, executes playbooks.
Information about current/emerging threats, TTPs, IoCs; informs defense strategies.
Proactively searching for signs of compromise/malicious activity in network.
Attack phases: Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, C2, Actions.
Framework documenting adversary tactics/techniques based on real-world observations.
Tactics: goals; Techniques: how-to; Procedures: specific implementation.
Regular training on security policies, phishing, password hygiene, social engineering.
Documents how users should use IT resources; defines prohibited activities.
Requirements: minimum length, complexity, expiration, history, account lockout after failures.
Uses biometrics, hardware keys, or other methods instead of passwords; more secure.
Fingerprint, facial recognition, iris scan; difficult to spoof; inherently non-transferable.
Distributed ledger technology; immutable records; applications in supply chain, smart contracts.
Future quantum computers can break RSA/ECC encryption; industry preparing post-quantum cryptography.
Input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, error handling, least privilege.
Ten most critical web security risks: injection, broken auth, XSS, broken access, misconfiguration, etc.
Peer examination of source code to find bugs, security issues, and ensure quality standards.
Analyzes source code without running it; finds vulnerabilities early in development.
Tests running application by sending malicious inputs; identifies runtime vulnerabilities.
Compliance: meets regulatory requirements; Security: defends against threats. Both important.
Independent review of security controls and practices against standards; documents findings.
Evaluation of security posture; identifies gaps and risks; informs improvement strategies.
Approved, documented standard configuration for systems; basis for compliance and detection.
Process for planning, testing, approving changes; minimizes disruption and unintended consequences.
Multiple overlapping security layers; if one fails, others provide protection.
Measurable indicators of security effectiveness: mean time to detect, patch rate, incident count.
Antivirus: primarily targets viruses; Antimalware: broader, targets all malware types.
Identifies malware by matching known patterns/signatures; fast but misses new/unknown threats.
Identifies malware by analyzing behavior patterns; detects unknown threats but prone to false positives.
Isolated environment for testing suspicious files/code safely without endangering real system.
Windows feature prompting admin approval for privileged actions; prevents unauthorized system changes.
Windows tool enforcing security policies across domain; controls settings on managed computers.
Windows feature restricting app execution; allows only approved applications to run.
Linux security module enforcing mandatory access control; provides granular permission control.
Recording system/security events for audit, investigation, and compliance purposes.
How long logs must be kept; depends on compliance requirements (usually 1-7 years).
Standard protocol for logging; forwards logs from devices to centralized logging server.
Network packet analyzer; captures and examines network traffic; useful for troubleshooting/investigation.
Network mapper; discovers hosts/services/OS on network; used for reconnaissance and vulnerability assessment.
Penetration testing framework; exploits vulnerabilities in systems; used by pentesters and attackers.
Vulnerability scanner; identifies missing patches, misconfigurations, compliance issues.
Open-source vulnerability scanner; similar to Nessus but free.
Wireless security auditing tools; cracks WPA/WEP encryption; assesses Wi-Fi security.
Fast password cracking tool; tests password strength using GPU acceleration.
Password cracking tool; attempts to crack hashes using dictionary, brute force, or rainbow tables.
Web app security testing platform; intercepts/modifies HTTP traffic; finds web vulnerabilities.
Free web application security scanner; automated scanning and manual testing.
